items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: What is the difference between aerobic and muscular endurance?
items.correct: Aerobic endurance is the cardiovascular system's ability to supply oxygen; muscular endurance is a muscle's ability to contract repeatedly without fatigue.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same component.
items.random_wrong: Aerobic is for legs, muscular for arms.
items.why: Students treat both as 'being able to keep going'. Aerobic endurance is WHOLE-BODY cardio capacity; muscular endurance is LOCALISED to specific muscles (e.g., sit-up reps).

items.q: Pulmonary circulation transports blood between the heart and which organ?
items.why: It's Lungs. “Liver” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which response shifts blood toward working muscles during exercise?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Vascular shunt. “Bradycardia” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Stroke volume 75 ml, heart rate 80 bpm. What is cardiac output in ml/min?
items.why: The real figure is 6000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
